title: AI can understand what a task is really asking
description: An AI system can recover intent from messy instructions, isolate the real job to be done, and frame the task correctly before acting.

subQuestions.title: Can it infer the real goal from messy instructions?
subQuestions.description: Tests whether the system can recover underlying intent when requests are informal, cluttered, or only partially specified.
subQuestions.rationale: Current frontier systems are already strong at reconstructing user intent in ordinary text workflows, even when prompts are noisy or indirect.

proofItems.title: Benchmark suite: Task-goal inference from messy instructions
proofItems.shortExplanation: Hidden-answer prompt suites show strong recovery of user intent from indirect, noisy, and incomplete requests.

subQuestions.title: Can it identify missing constraints and ask clarifying questions?
subQuestions.description: Checks whether the system notices when a request is underspecified and asks the right follow-up before executing.
subQuestions.rationale: Ambiguity handling is now solid in many assistant workflows when the model is allowed to pause and clarify before acting.

proofItems.title: Controlled study: Clarification behavior on underspecified requests
proofItems.shortExplanation: Controlled user studies show high rates of useful follow-up questions before action on incomplete enterprise tasks.

subQuestions.title: Can it separate relevant from irrelevant information?
subQuestions.description: Measures whether the system can ignore distracting context and extract the task-critical variables.
subQuestions.rationale: Relevance filtering is competitive on many structured and semi-structured tasks, though high-noise adversarial settings still reduce accuracy.

proofItems.title: Leaderboard: Relevant-variable extraction under distracting context
proofItems.shortExplanation: Leaderboard evaluations show top systems retaining the right task variables even when prompts include decoys and irrelevant detail.

subQuestions.title: Can it map ambiguous requests to the right task structure?
subQuestions.description: Tests whether the system chooses the correct frame for planning, retrieval, analysis, generation, or execution.
subQuestions.rationale: Models often choose the right frame, but still misclassify edge cases where requests could plausibly be handled in more than one way.

proofItems.title: Expert blind review: Task-frame selection for ambiguous user requests
proofItems.source: Expert Review Panel on AI Performance
proofItems.shortExplanation: Expert raters judged many outputs well-framed, but noted persistent failures on ambiguous planning-versus-retrieval boundaries.

subQuestions.title: Can it keep the same interpretation across paraphrases?
subQuestions.description: Looks for stable task understanding when the same request is reworded in different ways.
subQuestions.rationale: Paraphrase stability has improved materially on broad assistant tasks, especially where the intent is ordinary and the context is clean.

proofItems.title: Benchmark suite: Paraphrase-stable task interpretation
proofItems.shortExplanation: Repeated-query evaluation sets show stable intent interpretation across alternate phrasings of the same user goal.
